html:
XXVII

Fond wretch! and what canst thou relate,
But deeds of sorrow, shame, and sin?
Thy deeds are provedвБ†вАФthou knowвАЩst thy fate;
But come, thy taleвБ†вАФbeginвБ†вАФbegin.

But I have griefs of other kind,
Troubles and sorrows more severe;
Give me to ease my tortured mind,
Lend to my woes a patient ear;
And let me, if I may not find
A friend to helpвБ†вАФfind one to hear.

CrabbeвАЩs Hall of Justice

When Urfried had with clamours and menaces driven Rebecca back to the apartment from which she had sallied, she proceeded to conduct the unwilling Cedric into a small apartment, the door of which she heedfully secured. Then fetching from a cupboard a stoup of wine and two flagons, she placed them on the table, and said in a tone rather asserting a fact than asking a question, вАЬThou art Saxon, fatherвБ†вАФDeny it not,вАЭ she continued, observing that Cedric hastened not to reply; вАЬthe sounds of my native language are sweet to mine ears, though seldom heard save from the tongues of the wretched and degraded serfs on whom the proud Normans impose the meanest drudgery of this dwelling. Thou art a Saxon, fatherвБ†вАФa Saxon, and, save as thou art a servant of God, a freeman.вБ†вАФThine accents are sweet in mine ear.вАЭ

вАЬDo not Saxon priests visit this castle, then?вАЭ replied Cedric; вАЬit were, methinks, their duty to comfort the outcast and oppressed children of the soil.вАЭ

вАЬThey come notвБ†вАФor if they come, they better love to revel at the boards of their conquerors,вАЭ answered Urfried, вАЬthan to hear the groans of their countrymenвБ†вАФso, at least, report speaks of themвБ†вАФof myself I can say little. This castle, for ten years, has opened to no priest save the debauched Norman chaplain who partook the nightly revels of Front-de-Boeuf, and he has been long gone to render an account of his stewardship.вБ†вАФBut thou art a SaxonвБ†вАФa Saxon priest, and I have one question to ask of thee.вАЭ

вАЬI am a Saxon,вАЭ answered Cedric, вАЬbut unworthy, surely, of the name of priest. Let me begone on my wayвБ†вАФI swear I will return, or send one of our fathers more worthy to hear your confession.вАЭ

вАЬStay yet a while,вАЭ said Urfried; вАЬthe accents of the voice which thou hearest now will soon be choked with the cold earth, and I would not descend to it like the beast I have lived. But wine must give me strength to tell the horrors of my tale.вАЭ She poured out a cup, and drank it with a frightful avidity, which seemed desirous of draining the last drop in the goblet. вАЬIt stupifies,вАЭ she said, looking upwards as she finished her drought, вАЬbut it cannot cheerвБ†вАФPartake it, father, if you would hear my tale without sinking down upon the pavement.вАЭ Cedric would have avoided pledging her in this ominous conviviality, but the sign which she made to him expressed impatience and despair. He complied with her request, and answered her challenge in a large wine-cup; she then proceeded with her story, as if appeased by his complaisance.

вАЬI was not born,вАЭ she said, вАЬfather, the wretch that thou now seest me. I was free, was happy, was honoured, loved, and was beloved. I am now a slave, miserable and degradedвБ†вАФthe sport of my mastersвАЩ passions while I had yet beautyвБ†вАФthe object of their contempt, scorn, and hatred, since it has passed away. Dost thou wonder, father, that I should hate mankind, and, above all, the race that has wrought this change in me? Can the wrinkled decrepit hag before thee, whose wrath must vent itself in impotent curses, forget she was once the daughter of the noble Thane of Torquilstone, before whose frown a thousand vassals trembled?вАЭ

вАЬThou the daughter of Torquil Wolfganger!вАЭ said Cedric, receding as he spoke; вАЬthouвБ†вАФthouвБ†вАФthe daughter of that noble Saxon, my fatherвАЩs friend and companion in arms!вАЭ

вАЬThy fatherвАЩs friend!вАЭ echoed Urfried; вАЬthen Cedric called the Saxon stands before me, for the noble Hereward of Rotherwood had but one son, whose name is well known among his countrymen. But if thou art Cedric of Rotherwood, why this religious dress?вБ†вАФhast thou too despaired of saving thy country, and sought refuge from oppression in the shade of the convent?вАЭ

вАЬIt matters not who I am,вАЭ said Cedric; вАЬproceed, unhappy woman, with thy tale of horror and guilt!вБ†вАФGuilt there must beвБ†вАФthere is guilt even in thy living to tell it.вАЭ

вАЬThere isвБ†вАФthere is,вАЭ answered the wretched woman, вАЬdeep, black, damning guiltвБ†вАФguilt, that lies like a load at my breastвБ†вАФguilt, that all the penitential fires of hereafter cannot cleanse.вБ†вАФYes, in these halls, stained with the noble and pure blood of my father and my brethrenвБ†вАФin these very halls, to have lived the paramour of their murderer, the slave at once and the partaker of his pleasures, was to render every breath which I drew of vital air, a crime and a curse.вАЭ

вАЬWretched woman!вАЭ exclaimed Cedric. вАЬAnd while the friends of thy fatherвБ†вАФwhile each true Saxon heart, as it breathed a requiem for his soul, and those of his valiant sons, forgot not in their prayers the murdered UlricaвБ†вАФwhile all mourned and honoured the dead, thou hast lived to merit our hate and execrationвБ†вАФlived to unite thyself with the vile tyrant who murdered thy nearest and dearestвБ†вАФwho shed the blood of infancy, rather than a male of the noble house of Torquil Wolfganger should surviveвБ†вАФwith him hast thou lived to unite thyself, and in the hands of lawless love!вАЭ

вАЬIn lawless hands, indeed, but not in those of love!вАЭ answered the hag; вАЬlove will sooner visit the regions of eternal doom, than those unhallowed vaults.вБ†вАФNo, with that at least I cannot reproach myselfвБ†вАФhatred to Front-de-Boeuf and his race governed my soul most deeply, even in the hour of his guilty endearments.вАЭ

вАЬYou hated him, and yet you lived,вАЭ replied Cedric; вАЬwretch! was there no poniardвБ†вАФno knifeвБ†вАФno bodkin!вБ†вАФWell was it for thee, since thou didst prize such an existence, that the secrets of a Norman castle are like those of the grave. For had I but dreamed of the daughter of Torquil living in foul communion with the murderer of her father, the sword of a true Saxon had found thee out even in the arms of thy paramour!вАЭ

вАЬWouldst thou indeed have done this justice to the name of Torquil?вАЭ said Ulrica, for we may now lay aside her assumed name of Urfried; вАЬthou art then the true Saxon report speaks thee! for even within these accursed walls, where, as thou well sayest, guilt shrouds itself in inscrutable mystery, even there has the name of Cedric been soundedвБ†вАФand I, wretched and degraded, have rejoiced to think that there yet breathed an avenger of our unhappy nation.вБ†вАФI also have had my hours of vengeanceвБ†вАФI have fomented the quarrels of our foes, and heated drunken revelry into murderous broilвБ†вАФI have seen their blood flowвБ†вАФI have heard their dying groans!вБ†вАФLook on me, CedricвБ†вАФare there not still left on this foul and faded face some traces of the features of Torquil?вАЭ

вАЬAsk me not of them, Ulrica,вАЭ replied Cedric, in a tone of grief mixed with abhorrence; вАЬthese traces form such a resemblance as arises from the graves of the dead, when a fiend has animated the lifeless corpse.вАЭ

вАЬBe it so,вАЭ answered Ulrica; вАЬyet wore these fiendish features the mask of a spirit of light when they were able to set at variance the elder Front-de-Boeuf and his son Reginald! The darkness of hell should hide what followed, but revenge must lift the veil, and darkly intimate what it would raise the dead to speak aloud. Long had the smouldering fire of discord glowed between the tyrant father and his savage sonвБ†вАФlong had I nursed, in secret, the unnatural hatredвБ†вАФit blazed forth in an hour of drunken wassail, and at his own board fell my oppressor by the hand of his own sonвБ†вАФsuch are the secrets these vaults conceal!вБ†вАФRend asunder, ye accursed arches,вАЭ she added, looking up towards the roof, вАЬand bury in your fall all who are conscious of the hideous mystery!вАЭ

вАЬAnd thou, creature of guilt and misery,вАЭ said Cedric, вАЬwhat became thy lot on the death of thy ravisher?вАЭ

вАЬGuess it, but ask it not.вБ†вАФHereвБ†вАФhere I dwelt, till age, premature age, has stamped its ghastly features on my countenanceвБ†вАФscorned and insulted where I was once obeyed, and compelled to bound the revenge which had once such ample scope, to the efforts of petty malice of a discontented menial, or the vain or unheeded curses of an impotent hagвБ†вАФcondemned to hear from my lonely turret the sounds of revelry in which I once partook, or the shrieks and groans of new victims of oppression.вАЭ

вАЬUlrica,вАЭ said Cedric, вАЬwith a heart which still, I fear, regrets the lost reward of thy crimes, as much as the deeds by which thou didst acquire that meed, how didst thou dare to address thee to one who wears this robe? Consider, unhappy woman, what could the sainted Edward himself do for thee, were he here in bodily presence? The royal Confessor was endowed by heaven with power to cleanse the ulcers of the body, but only God himself can cure the leprosy of the soul.вАЭ

вАЬYet, turn not from me, stern prophet of wrath,вАЭ she exclaimed, вАЬbut tell me, if thou canst, in what shall terminate these new and awful feelings that burst on my solitudeвБ†вАФWhy do deeds, long since done, rise before me in new and irresistible horrors? What fate is prepared beyond the grave for her, to whom God has assigned on earth a lot of such unspeakable wretchedness? Better had I turn to Woden, Hertha, and ZernebockвБ†вАФto Mista, and to Skogula, the gods of our yet unbaptized ancestors, than endure the dreadful anticipations which have of late haunted my waking and my sleeping hours!вАЭ

вАЬI am no priest,вАЭ said Cedric, turning with disgust from this miserable picture of guilt, wretchedness, and despair; вАЬI am no priest, though I wear a priestвАЩs garment.вАЭ

вАЬPriest or layman,вАЭ answered Ulrica, вАЬthou art the first I have seen for twenty years, by whom God was feared or man regarded; and dost thou bid me despair?вАЭ

вАЬI bid thee repent,вАЭ said Cedric. вАЬSeek to prayer and penance, and mayest thou find acceptance! But I cannot, I will not, longer abide with thee.вАЭ

вАЬStay yet a moment!вАЭ said Ulrica; вАЬleave me not now, son of my fatherвАЩs friend, lest the demon who has governed my life should tempt me to avenge myself of thy hard-hearted scornвБ†вАФThinkest thou, if Front-de-Boeuf found Cedric the Saxon in his castle, in such a disguise, that thy life would be a long one?вБ†вАФAlready his eye has been upon thee like a falcon on his prey.вАЭ

вАЬAnd be it so,вАЭ said Cedric; вАЬand let him tear me with beak and talons, ere my tongue say one word which my heart doth not warrant. I will die a SaxonвБ†вАФtrue in word, open in deedвБ†вАФI bid thee avaunt!вБ†вАФtouch me not, stay me not!вБ†вАФThe sight of Front-de-Boeuf himself is less odious to me than thou, degraded and degenerate as thou art.вАЭ

вАЬBe it so,вАЭ said Ulrica, no longer interrupting him; вАЬgo thy way, and forget, in the insolence of thy superority, that the wretch before thee is the daughter of thy fatherвАЩs friend.вБ†вАФGo thy wayвБ†вАФif I am separated from mankind by my sufferingsвБ†вАФseparated from those whose aid I might most justly expectвБ†вАФnot less will I be separated from them in my revenge!вБ†вАФNo man shall aid me, but the ears of all men shall tingle to hear of the deed which I shall dare to do!вБ†вАФFarewell!вБ†вАФthy scorn has burst the last tie which seemed yet to unite me to my kindвБ†вАФa thought that my woes might claim the compassion of my people.вАЭ

вАЬUlrica,вАЭ said Cedric, softened by this appeal, вАЬhast thou borne up and endured to live through so much guilt and so much misery, and wilt thou now yield to despair when thine eyes are opened to thy crimes, and when repentance were thy fitter occupation?вАЭ

вАЬCedric,вАЭ answered Ulrica, вАЬthou little knowest the human heart. To act as I have acted, to think as I have thought, requires the maddening love of pleasure, mingled with the keen appetite of revenge, the proud consciousness of power; droughts too intoxicating for the human heart to bear, and yet retain the power to prevent. Their force has long passed awayвБ†вАФAge has no pleasures, wrinkles have no influence, revenge itself dies away in impotent curses. Then comes remorse, with all its vipers, mixed with vain regrets for the past, and despair for the future!вБ†вАФThen, when all other strong impulses have ceased, we become like the fiends in hell, who may feel remorse, but never repentance.вБ†вАФBut thy words have awakened a new soul within meвБ†вАФWell hast thou said, all is possible for those who dare to die!вБ†вАФThou hast shown me the means of revenge, and be assured I will embrace them. It has hitherto shared this wasted bosom with other and with rival passionsвБ†вАФhenceforward it shall possess me wholly, and thou thyself shalt say, that, whatever was the life of Ulrica, her death well became the daughter of the noble Torquil. There is a force without beleaguering this accursed castleвБ†вАФhasten to lead them to the attack, and when thou shalt see a red flag wave from the turret on the eastern angle of the donjon, press the Normans hardвБ†вАФthey will then have enough to do within, and you may win the wall in spite both of bow and mangonel.вБ†вАФBegone, I pray theeвБ†вАФfollow thine own fate, and leave me to mine.вАЭ

Cedric would have enquired farther into the purpose which she thus darkly announced, but the stern voice of Front-de-Boeuf was heard, exclaiming, вАЬWhere tarries this loitering priest? By the scallop-shell of Compostella, I will make a martyr of him, if he loiters here to hatch treason among my domestics!вАЭ

вАЬWhat a true prophet,вАЭ said Ulrica, вАЬis an evil conscience! But heed him notвБ†вАФout and to thy peopleвБ†вАФCry your Saxon onslaught, and let them sing their war-song of Rollo, if they will; vengeance shall bear a burden to it.вАЭ

As she thus spoke, she vanished through a private door, and Reginald Front-de-Boeuf entered the apartment. Cedric, with some difficulty, compelled himself to make obeisance to the haughty Baron, who returned his courtesy with a slight inclination of the head.

вАЬThy penitents, father, have made a long shriftвБ†вАФit is the better for them, since it is the last they shall ever make. Hast thou prepared them for death?вАЭ

вАЬI found them,вАЭ said Cedric, in such French as he could command, вАЬexpecting the worst, from the moment they knew into whose power they had fallen.вАЭ

вАЬHow now, Sir Friar,вАЭ replied Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬthy speech, methinks, smacks of a Saxon tongue?вАЭ

вАЬI was bred in the convent of St.¬†Withold of Burton,вАЭ answered Cedric.

вАЬAy?вАЭ said the Baron; вАЬit had been better for thee to have been a Norman, and better for my purpose too; but need has no choice of messengers. That St.¬†WitholdвАЩs of Burton is an owletвАЩs nest worth the harrying. The day will soon come that the frock shall protect the Saxon as little as the mail-coat.вАЭ

вАЬGodвАЩs will be done,вАЭ said Cedric, in a voice tremulous with passion, which Front-de-Boeuf imputed to fear.

вАЬI see,вАЭ said he, вАЬthou dreamest already that our men-at-arms are in thy refectory and thy ale-vaults. But do me one cast of thy holy office, and, come what list of others, thou shalt sleep as safe in thy cell as a snail within his shell of proof.вАЭ

вАЬSpeak your commands,вАЭ said Cedric, with suppressed emotion.

вАЬFollow me through this passage, then, that I may dismiss thee by the postern.вАЭ

And as he strode on his way before the supposed friar, Front-de-Boeuf thus schooled him in the part which he desired he should act.

вАЬThou seest, Sir Friar, yon herd of Saxon swine, who have dared to environ this castle of TorquilstoneвБ†вАФTell them whatever thou hast a mind of the weakness of this fortalice, or aught else that can detain them before it for twenty-four hours. Meantime bear thou this scrollвБ†вАФBut softвБ†вАФcanst read, Sir Priest?вАЭ

вАЬNot a jot I,вАЭ answered Cedric, вАЬsave on my breviary; and then I know the characters, because I have the holy service by heart, praised be Our Lady and St.¬†Withold!вАЭ

вАЬThe fitter messenger for my purpose.вБ†вАФCarry thou this scroll to the castle of Philip de Malvoisin; say it cometh from me, and is written by the Templar Brian de Bois-Guilbert, and that I pray him to send it to York with all the speed man and horse can make. Meanwhile, tell him to doubt nothing, he shall find us whole and sound behind our battlementвБ†вАФShame on it, that we should be compelled to hide thus by a pack of runagates, who are wont to fly even at the flash of our pennons and the tramp of our horses! I say to thee, priest, contrive some cast of thine art to keep the knaves where they are, until our friends bring up their lances. My vengeance is awake, and she is a falcon that slumbers not till she has been gorged.вАЭ

вАЬBy my patron saint,вАЭ said Cedric, with deeper energy than became his character, вАЬand by every saint who has lived and died in England, your commands shall be obeyed! Not a Saxon shall stir from before these walls, if I have art and influence to detain them there.вАЭ

вАЬHa!вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬthou changest thy tone, Sir Priest, and speakest brief and bold, as if thy heart were in the slaughter of the Saxon herd; and yet thou art thyself of kindred to the swine?вАЭ

Cedric was no ready practiser of the art of dissimulation, and would at this moment have been much the better of a hint from WambaвАЩs more fertile brain. But necessity, according to the ancient proverb, sharpens invention, and he muttered something under his cowl concerning the men in question being excommunicated outlaws both to church and to kingdom.

вАЬDespardieux,вАЭ answered Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬthou hast spoken the very truthвБ†вАФI forgot that the knaves can strip a fat abbot, as well as if they had been born south of yonder salt channel. Was it not he of St.¬†Ives whom they tied to an oak-tree, and compelled to sing a mass while they were rifling his mails and his wallets?вБ†вАФNo, by our LadyвБ†вАФthat jest was played by Gualtier of Middleton, one of our own companions-at-arms. But they were Saxons who robbed the chapel at St.¬†Bees of cup, candlestick and chalice, were they not?вАЭ

вАЬThey were godless men,вАЭ answered Cedric.

вАЬAy, and they drank out all the good wine and ale that lay in store for many a secret carousal, when ye pretend ye are but busied with vigils and primes!вБ†вАФPriest, thou art bound to revenge such sacrilege.вАЭ

вАЬI am indeed bound to vengeance,вАЭ murmured Cedric; вАЬSaint Withold knows my heart.вАЭ

Front-de-Boeuf, in the meanwhile, led the way to a postern, where, passing the moat on a single plank, they reached a small barbican, or exterior defence, which communicated with the open field by a well-fortified sallyport.

вАЬBegone, then; and if thou wilt do mine errand, and if thou return hither when it is done, thou shalt see Saxon flesh cheap as ever was hogвАЩs in the shambles of Sheffield. And, hark thee, thou seemest to be a jolly confessorвБ†вАФcome hither after the onslaught, and thou shalt have as much Malvoisie as would drench thy whole convent.вАЭ

вАЬAssuredly we shall meet again,вАЭ answered Cedric.

вАЬSomething in hand the whilst,вАЭ continued the Norman; and, as they parted at the postern door, he thrust into CedricвАЩs reluctant hand a gold byzant, adding, вАЬRemember, I will fly off both cowl and skin, if thou failest in thy purpose.вАЭ

вАЬAnd full leave will I give thee to do both,вАЭ answered Cedric, leaving the postern, and striding forth over the free field with a joyful step, вАЬif, when we meet next, I deserve not better at thine hand.вАЭвБ†вАФTurning then back towards the castle, he threw the piece of gold towards the donor, exclaiming at the same time, вАЬFalse Norman, thy money perish with thee!вАЭ

Front-de-Boeuf heard the words imperfectly, but the action was suspiciousвБ†вАФвАЬArchers,вАЭ he called to the warders on the outward battlements, вАЬsend me an arrow through yon monkвАЩs frock!вБ†вАФyet stay,вАЭ he said, as his retainers were bending their bows, вАЬit avails notвБ†вАФwe must thus far trust him since we have no better shift. I think he dares not betray meвБ†вАФat the worst I can but treat with these Saxon dogs whom I have safe in kennel.вБ†вАФHo! Giles jailor, let them bring Cedric of Rotherwood before me, and the other churl, his companionвБ†вАФhim I mean of ConingsburghвБ†вАФAthelstane there, or what call they him? Their very names are an encumbrance to a Norman knightвАЩs mouth, and have, as it were, a flavour of baconвБ†вАФGive me a stoup of wine, as jolly Prince John said, that I may wash away the relishвБ†вАФplace it in the armoury, and thither lead the prisoners.вАЭ

His commands were obeyed; and, upon entering that Gothic apartment, hung with many spoils won by his own valour and that of his father, he found a flagon of wine on the massive oaken table, and the two Saxon captives under the guard of four of his dependants. Front-de-Boeuf took a long drought of wine, and then addressed his prisoners;вБ†вАФfor the manner in which Wamba drew the cap over his face, the change of dress, the gloomy and broken light, and the BaronвАЩs imperfect acquaintance with the features of Cedric, (who avoided his Norman neighbours, and seldom stirred beyond his own domains,) prevented him from discovering that the most important of his captives had made his escape.

вАЬGallants of England,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬhow relish ye your entertainment at Torquilstone?вБ†вАФAre ye yet aware what your surquedy and outrecuidance30 merit, for scoffing at the entertainment of a prince of the House of Anjou?вБ†вАФHave ye forgotten how ye requited the unmerited hospitality of the royal John? By God and St.¬†Dennis, an ye pay not the richer ransom, I will hang ye up by the feet from the iron bars of these windows, till the kites and hooded crows have made skeletons of you!вБ†вАФSpeak out, ye Saxon dogsвБ†вАФwhat bid ye for your worthless lives?вБ†вАФHow say you, you of Rotherwood?вАЭ

вАЬNot a doit I,вАЭ answered poor WambaвБ†вАФвАЬand for hanging up by the feet, my brain has been topsy-turvy, they say, ever since the biggin was bound first round my head; so turning me upside down may peradventure restore it again.вАЭ

вАЬSaint Genevieve!вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬwhat have we got here?вАЭ

And with the back of his hand he struck CedricвАЩs cap from the head of the Jester, and throwing open his collar, discovered the fatal badge of servitude, the silver collar round his neck.

вАЬGilesвБ†вАФClementвБ†вАФdogs and varlets!вАЭ exclaimed the furious Norman, вАЬwhat have you brought me here?вАЭ

вАЬI think I can tell you,вАЭ said De Bracy, who just entered the apartment. вАЬThis is CedricвАЩs clown, who fought so manful a skirmish with Isaac of York about a question of precedence.вАЭ

вАЬI shall settle it for them both,вАЭ replied Front-de-Boeuf; вАЬthey shall hang on the same gallows, unless his master and this boar of Coningsburgh will pay well for their lives. Their wealth is the least they can surrender; they must also carry off with them the swarms that are besetting the castle, subscribe a surrender of their pretended immunities, and live under us as serfs and vassals; too happy if, in the new world that is about to begin, we leave them the breath of their nostrils.вБ†вАФGo,вАЭ said he to two of his attendants, вАЬfetch me the right Cedric hither, and I pardon your error for once; the rather that you but mistook a fool for a Saxon franklin.вАЭ

вАЬAy, but,вАЭ said Wamba, вАЬyour chivalrous excellency will find there are more fools than franklins among us.вАЭ

вАЬWhat means the knave?вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, looking towards his followers, who, lingering and loath, faltered forth their belief, that if this were not Cedric who was there in presence, they knew not what was become of him.

вАЬSaints of Heaven!вАЭ exclaimed De Bracy, вАЬhe must have escaped in the monkвАЩs garments!вАЭ

вАЬFiends of hell!вАЭ echoed Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬit was then the boar of Rotherwood whom I ushered to the postern, and dismissed with my own hands!вБ†вАФAnd thou,вАЭ he said to Wamba, вАЬwhose folly could overreach the wisdom of idiots yet more gross than thyselfвБ†вАФI will give thee holy ordersвБ†вАФI will shave thy crown for thee!вБ†вАФHere, let them tear the scalp from his head, and then pitch him headlong from the battlementsвБ†вАФThy trade is to jest, canst thou jest now?вАЭ

вАЬYou deal with me better than your word, noble knight,вАЭ whimpered forth poor Wamba, whose habits of buffoonery were not to be overcome even by the immediate prospect of death; вАЬif you give me the red cap you propose, out of a simple monk you will make a cardinal.вАЭ

вАЬThe poor wretch,вАЭ said De Bracy, вАЬis resolved to die in his vocation.вБ†вАФFront-de-Boeuf, you shall not slay him. Give him to me to make sport for my Free Companions.вБ†вАФHow sayst thou, knave? Wilt thou take heart of grace, and go to the wars with me?вАЭ

вАЬAy, with my masterвАЩs leave,вАЭ said Wamba; вАЬfor, look you, I must not slip collarвАЭ (and he touched that which he wore) вАЬwithout his permission.вАЭ

вАЬOh, a Norman saw will soon cut a Saxon collar,вАЭ said De Bracy.

вАЬAy, noble sir,вАЭ said Wamba, вАЬand thence goes the proverbвБ†вАФ

вАШNorman saw on English oak,
On English neck a Norman yoke;
Norman spoon in English dish,
And England ruled as Normans wish;
Blithe world to England never will be more,
Till EnglandвАЩs rid of all the four.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬThou dost well, De Bracy,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬto stand there listening to a foolвАЩs jargon, when destruction is gaping for us! Seest thou not we are overreached, and that our proposed mode of communicating with our friends without has been disconcerted by this same motley gentleman thou art so fond to brother? What views have we to expect but instant storm?вАЭ

вАЬTo the battlements then,вАЭ said De Bracy; вАЬwhen didst thou ever see me the graver for the thoughts of battle? Call the Templar yonder, and let him fight but half so well for his life as he has done for his OrderвБ†вАФMake thou to the walls thyself with thy huge bodyвБ†вАФLet me do my poor endeavour in my own way, and I tell thee the Saxon outlaws may as well attempt to scale the clouds, as the castle of Torquilstone; or, if you will treat with the banditti, why not employ the mediation of this worthy franklin, who seems in such deep contemplation of the wine-flagon?вБ†вАФHere, Saxon,вАЭ he continued, addressing Athelstane, and handing the cup to him, вАЬrinse thy throat with that noble liquor, and rouse up thy soul to say what thou wilt do for thy liberty.вАЭ

вАЬWhat a man of mould may,вАЭ answered Athelstane, вАЬproviding it be what a man of manhood ought.вБ†вАФDismiss me free, with my companions, and I will pay a ransom of a thousand marks.вАЭ

вАЬAnd wilt moreover assure us the retreat of that scum of mankind who are swarming around the castle, contrary to GodвАЩs peace and the kingвАЩs?вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf.

вАЬIn so far as I can,вАЭ answered Athelstane, вАЬI will withdraw them; and I fear not but that my father Cedric will do his best to assist me.вАЭ

вАЬWe are agreed then,вАЭ said Front-de-BoeufвБ†вАФвАЬthou and they are to be set at freedom, and peace is to be on both sides, for payment of a thousand marks. It is a trifling ransom, Saxon, and thou wilt owe gratitude to the moderation which accepts of it in exchange of your persons. But mark, this extends not to the Jew Isaac.вАЭ

вАЬNor to the Jew IsaacвАЩs daughter,вАЭ said the Templar, who had now joined them.

вАЬNeither,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬbelong to this SaxonвАЩs company.вАЭ

вАЬI were unworthy to be called Christian, if they did,вАЭ replied Athelstane: вАЬdeal with the unbelievers as ye list.вАЭ

вАЬNeither does the ransom include the Lady Rowena,вАЭ said De Bracy. вАЬIt shall never be said I was scared out of a fair prize without striking a blow for it.вАЭ

вАЬNeither,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬdoes our treaty refer to this wretched Jester, whom I retain, that I may make him an example to every knave who turns jest into earnest.вАЭ

вАЬThe Lady Rowena,вАЭ answered Athelstane, with the most steady countenance, вАЬis my affianced bride. I will be drawn by wild horses before I consent to part with her. The slave Wamba has this day saved the life of my father CedricвБ†вАФI will lose mine ere a hair of his head be injured.вАЭ

вАЬThy affianced bride?вБ†вАФThe Lady Rowena the affianced bride of a vassal like thee?вАЭ said De Bracy; вАЬSaxon, thou dreamest that the days of thy seven kingdoms are returned again. I tell thee, the Princes of the House of Anjou confer not their wards on men of such lineage as thine.вАЭ

вАЬMy lineage, proud Norman,вАЭ replied Athelstane, вАЬis drawn from a source more pure and ancient than that of a beggarly Frenchman, whose living is won by selling the blood of the thieves whom he assembles under his paltry standard. Kings were my ancestors, strong in war and wise in council, who every day feasted in their hall more hundreds than thou canst number individual followers; whose names have been sung by minstrels, and their laws recorded by Wittenagemotes; whose bones were interred amid the prayers of saints, and over whose tombs minsters have been builded.вАЭ

вАЬThou hast it, De Bracy,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, well pleased with the rebuff which his companion had received; вАЬthe Saxon hath hit thee fairly.вАЭ

вАЬAs fairly as a captive can strike,вАЭ said De Bracy, with apparent carelessness; вАЬfor he whose hands are tied should have his tongue at freedom.вБ†вАФBut thy glibness of reply, comrade,вАЭ rejoined he, speaking to Athelstane, вАЬwill not win the freedom of the Lady Rowena.вАЭ

To this Athelstane, who had already made a longer speech than was his custom to do on any topic, however interesting, returned no answer. The conversation was interrupted by the arrival of a menial, who announced that a monk demanded admittance at the postern gate.

вАЬIn the name of Saint Bennet, the prince of these bull-beggars,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬhave we a real monk this time, or another impostor? Search him, slavesвБ†вАФfor an ye suffer a second impostor to be palmed upon you, I will have your eyes torn out, and hot coals put into the sockets.вАЭ

вАЬLet me endure the extremity of your anger, my lord,вАЭ said Giles, вАЬif this be not a real shaveling. Your squire Jocelyn knows him well, and will vouch him to be brother Ambrose, a monk in attendance upon the Prior of Jorvaulx.вАЭ

вАЬAdmit him,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf; вАЬmost likely he brings us news from his jovial master. Surely the devil keeps holiday, and the priests are relieved from duty, that they are strolling thus wildly through the country. Remove these prisoners; and, Saxon, think on what thou hast heard.вАЭ

вАЬI claim,вАЭ said Athelstane, вАЬan honourable imprisonment, with due care of my board and of my couch, as becomes my rank, and as is due to one who is in treaty for ransom. Moreover, I hold him that deems himself the best of you, bound to answer to me with his body for this aggression on my freedom. This defiance hath already been sent to thee by thy sewer; thou underliest it, and art bound to answer meвБ†вАФThere lies my glove.вАЭ

вАЬI answer not the challenge of my prisoner,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf; вАЬnor shalt thou, Maurice de Bracy.вБ†вАФGiles,вАЭ he continued, вАЬhang the franklinвАЩs glove upon the tine of yonder branched antlers: there shall it remain until he is a free man. Should he then presume to demand it, or to affirm he was unlawfully made my prisoner, by the belt of Saint Christopher, he will speak to one who hath never refused to meet a foe on foot or on horseback, alone or with his vassals at his back!вАЭ

The Saxon prisoners were accordingly removed, just as they introduced the monk Ambrose, who appeared to be in great perturbation.

вАЬThis is the real deus vobiscum,вАЭ said Wamba, as he passed the reverend brother; вАЬthe others were but counterfeits.вАЭ

вАЬHoly Mother,вАЭ said the monk, as he addressed the assembled knights, вАЬI am at last safe and in Christian keeping!вАЭ

вАЬSafe thou art,вАЭ replied De Bracy; вАЬand for Christianity, here is the stout Baron Reginald Front-de-Boeuf, whose utter abomination is a Jew; and the good Knight Templar, Brian de Bois-Guilbert, whose trade is to slay SaracensвБ†вАФIf these are not good marks of Christianity, I know no other which they bear about them.вАЭ

вАЬYe are friends and allies of our reverend father in God, Aymer, Prior of Jorvaulx,вАЭ said the monk, without noticing the tone of De BracyвАЩs reply; вАЬye owe him aid both by knightly faith and holy charity; for what saith the blessed Saint Augustin, in his treatise De Civitate DeiвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬWhat saith the devil!вАЭ interrupted Front-de-Boeuf; вАЬor rather what dost thou say, Sir Priest? We have little time to hear texts from the holy fathers.вАЭ

вАЬSancta Maria!вАЭ ejaculated Father Ambrose, вАЬhow prompt to ire are these unhallowed laymen!вБ†вАФBut be it known to you, brave knights, that certain murderous caitiffs, casting behind them fear of God, and reverence of his church, and not regarding the bull of the holy see, Si quis, suadende DiaboloвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬBrother priest,вАЭ said the Templar, вАЬall this we know or guess atвБ†вАФtell us plainly, is thy master, the Prior, made prisoner, and to whom?вАЭ

вАЬSurely,вАЭ said Ambrose, вАЬhe is in the hands of the men of Belial, infesters of these woods, and contemners of the holy text, вАШTouch not mine anointed, and do my prophets naught of evil.вАЩвАКвАЭ

вАЬHere is a new argument for our swords, sirs,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, turning to his companions; вАЬand so, instead of reaching us any assistance, the Prior of Jorvaulx requests aid at our hands? a man is well helped of these lazy churchmen when he hath most to do!вБ†вАФBut speak out, priest, and say at once, what doth thy master expect from us?вАЭ

вАЬSo please you,вАЭ said Ambrose, вАЬviolent hands having been imposed on my reverend superior, contrary to the holy ordinance which I did already quote, and the men of Belial having rifled his mails and budgets, and stripped him of two hundred marks of pure refined gold, they do yet demand of him a large sum beside, ere they will suffer him to depart from their uncircumcised hands. Wherefore the reverend father in God prays you, as his dear friends, to rescue him, either by paying down the ransom at which they hold him, or by force of arms, at your best discretion.вАЭ

вАЬThe foul fiend quell the Prior!вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf; вАЬhis morningвАЩs drought has been a deep one. When did thy master hear of a Norman baron unbuckling his purse to relieve a churchman, whose bags are ten times as weighty as ours?вБ†вАФAnd how can we do aught by valour to free him, that are cooped up here by ten times our number, and expect an assault every moment?вАЭ

вАЬAnd that was what I was about to tell you,вАЭ said the monk, вАЬhad your hastiness allowed me time. But, God help me, I am old, and these foul onslaughts distract an aged manвАЩs brain. Nevertheless, it is of verity that they assemble a camp, and raise a bank against the walls of this castle.вАЭ

вАЬTo the battlements!вАЭ cried De Bracy, вАЬand let us mark what these knaves do without;вАЭ and so saying, he opened a latticed window which led to a sort of bartisan or projecting balcony, and immediately called from thence to those in the apartmentвБ†вАФвАЬSaint Dennis, but the old monk hath brought true tidings!вБ†вАФThey bring forward mantelets and pavisses,31 and the archers muster on the skirts of the wood like a dark cloud before a hailstorm.вАЭ

Reginald Front-de-Boeuf also looked out upon the field, and immediately snatched his bugle; and, after winding a long and loud blast, commanded his men to their posts on the walls.

вАЬDe Bracy, look to the eastern side, where the walls are lowestвБ†вАФNoble Bois-Guilbert, thy trade hath well taught thee how to attack and defend, look thou to the western sideвБ†вАФI myself will take post at the barbican. Yet, do not confine your exertions to any one spot, noble friends!вБ†вАФwe must this day be everywhere, and multiply ourselves, were it possible, so as to carry by our presence succour and relief wherever the attack is hottest. Our numbers are few, but activity and courage may supply that defect, since we have only to do with rascal clowns.вАЭ

вАЬBut, noble knights,вАЭ exclaimed Father Ambrose, amidst the bustle and confusion occasioned by the preparations for defence, вАЬwill none of ye hear the message of the reverend father in God Aymer, Prior of Jorvaulx?вБ†вАФI beseech thee to hear me, noble Sir Reginald!вАЭ

вАЬGo patter thy petitions to heaven,вАЭ said the fierce Norman, вАЬfor we on earth have no time to listen to them.вБ†вАФHo! there, Anselm I see that seething pitch and oil are ready to pour on the heads of these audacious traitorsвБ†вАФLook that the cross-bowmen lack not bolts.32вБ†вАФFling abroad my banner with the old bullвАЩs headвБ†вАФthe knaves shall soon find with whom they have to do this day!вАЭ

вАЬBut, noble sir,вАЭ continued the monk, persevering in his endeavours to draw attention, вАЬconsider my vow of obedience, and let me discharge myself of my SuperiorвАЩs errand.вАЭ

вАЬAway with this prating dotard,вАЭ said Front-de Boeuf, вАЬlock him up in the chapel, to tell his beads till the broil be over. It will be a new thing to the saints in Torquilstone to hear aves and paters; they have not been so honoured, I trow, since they were cut out of stone.вАЭ

вАЬBlaspheme not the holy saints, Sir Reginald,вАЭ said De Bracy, вАЬwe shall have need of their aid today before yon rascal rout disband.вАЭ

вАЬI expect little aid from their hand,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬunless we were to hurl them from the battlements on the heads of the villains. There is a huge lumbering Saint Christopher yonder, sufficient to bear a whole company to the earth.вАЭ

The Templar had in the meantime been looking out on the proceedings of the besiegers, with rather more attention than the brutal Front-de-Boeuf or his giddy companion.

вАЬBy the faith of mine order,вАЭ he said, вАЬthese men approach with more touch of discipline than could have been judged, however they come by it. See ye how dexterously they avail themselves of every cover which a tree or bush affords, and shun exposing themselves to the shot of our crossbows? I spy neither banner nor pennon among them, and yet will I gage my golden chain, that they are led on by some noble knight or gentleman, skilful in the practice of wars.вАЭ

вАЬI espy him,вАЭ said De Bracy; вАЬI see the waving of a knightвАЩs crest, and the gleam of his armour. See yon tall man in the black mail, who is busied marshalling the farther troop of the rascaille yeomenвБ†вАФby Saint Dennis, I hold him to be the same whom we called Le Noir Faineant, who overthrew thee, Front-de-Boeuf, in the lists at Ashby.вАЭ

вАЬSo much the better,вАЭ said Front-de-Boeuf, вАЬthat he comes here to give me my revenge. Some hilding fellow he must be, who dared not stay to assert his claim to the tourney prize which chance had assigned him. I should in vain have sought for him where knights and nobles seek their foes, and right glad am I he hath here shown himself among yon villain yeomanry.вАЭ

The demonstrations of the enemyвАЩs immediate approach cut off all farther discourse. Each knight repaired to his post, and at the head of the few followers whom they were able to muster, and who were in numbers inadequate to defend the whole extent of the walls, they awaited with calm determination the threatened assault.
